name: Qatar
tagline: From pearling shores and Bedouin tribes to a gas-rich peninsula state and global stage.
summary: Qatar is a small peninsula thrusting north into the Persian Gulf, long inhabited by pearl divers, fishermen and Bedouin tribes whose fortunes rose and fell with the pearl trade. The walled town of Al Zubarah flourished as a pearling and trading port in the late 18th century before its destruction in 1811. The Al Thani family rose to leadership over the peninsula in the 19th century, and a separate British agreement signed with Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani in 1868 is taken to mark Qatar's emergence as a distinct entity; a 1916 treaty made it a British protectorate. Oil discovered in 1939 and, later, the vast offshore North Field gas reserves transformed Qatar after independence on 3 September 1971 into one of the world's wealthiest states and a prominent diplomatic and media actor.

founding.label: Independence from Britain
founding.detail: Qatar declared its independence from Great Britain on 3 September 1971, ending the protectorate established by the 1916 Anglo-Qatari Treaty. The United States recognised the new State of Qatar two days later, on 5 September 1971, and the two established diplomatic relations in March 1972. Qatar's deeper roots as a polity are traced to the British agreement signed with Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani in 1868, which set the course for both Qatari autonomy and the rule of the Al Thani dynasty.

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Doha (Ad-Dawhah), on the east coast of the peninsula

quickFacts.label: Ruling dynasty
quickFacts.value: Al Thani family, paramount since the 19th century

quickFacts.label: National Day
quickFacts.value: 18 December, marking Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed's 1878 succession

quickFacts.label: Economic mainstay
quickFacts.value: Petroleum (from 1949) and liquefied natural gas from the North Field

eras.title: Pearls, Tribes and the Town of Al Zubarah
eras.standfirst: For millennia the peninsula's people lived from the sea and the desert, and in the 18th century the walled town of Al Zubarah grew into a flourishing pearling and trading port.
body: Before the 18th century the Qatari peninsula was a sparsely peopled land of small fishing settlements and Bedouin nomads who herded in the interior of Arabia and moved onto the peninsula chiefly in winter. Its arid coast offered little farming, but the warm, shallow waters of the Persian Gulf held some of the richest natural pearl-oyster beds in the world. Pearling and fishing, together with seasonal trade, sustained the coastal villages; pearls were the region's great export, carried by boat across the Indian Ocean and Arabia from the early Islamic period onward.

Qatar's modern history conventionally begins in the mid-18th century with the migration of families from Kuwait, among them the Al Khalifa, who settled on the northwest coast and built the town of Al Zubarah. Founded around the 1760s, Al Zubarah developed into the best-preserved example of an 18th- and 19th-century trading and pearl-fishing town in the Gulf, with palaces, mosques, courtyard houses, markets, a harbour, a canal and double defensive walls. It traded across the Indian Ocean, Arabia and western Asia and drew merchants and divers to its prosperous quarters.

Al Zubarah's wealth made it a target in the contests between Gulf powers. The town was burned and largely destroyed in 1811 amid regional conflict, and over the following century its harbour silted and its people drifted away, until it was finally abandoned in the first decades of the 20th century. Drifting desert sand sealed and protected its ruins, leaving an exceptional archaeological record of Gulf pearling society. In 2013 the Al Zubarah Archaeological Site became Qatar's first UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognised as outstanding testimony to the urban pearling tradition that shaped the region.

events.title: Pearling and fishing villages on the Gulf coast
events.summary: The peninsula's people lived from pearl diving, fishing and seasonal Bedouin herding, with pearls the great export carried across the Indian Ocean and Arabia from the early Islamic period and earlier.

events.title: Founding of Al Zubarah
events.summary: Families migrating from Kuwait, including the Al Khalifa, settled the northwest coast and built Al Zubarah, which grew into a flourishing walled pearling and trading town.

events.title: Destruction of Al Zubarah
events.summary: After attacks by rival Gulf powers, the thriving town of Al Zubarah was burned to the ground; it fell into decline and was abandoned by the early 20th century.

pullquote.text: The walled coastal town of Al Zubarah flourished as a pearling and trading centre before it was destroyed and abandoned.

eras.title: The Rise of the Al Thani and the 1868 Agreement
eras.standfirst: Amid disputes with Bahrain and Wahhabi pressure, Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani emerged as leader of the peninsula's tribes, and a separate British agreement in 1868 is taken to mark Qatar's emergence as a distinct entity.
body: In the mid-19th century the Al Thani, one of several leading families on the peninsula, rose to paramount leadership among the Qatari tribes under Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani. The peninsula was caught between competing powers: the Al Khalifa rulers of Bahrain claimed authority over Qatar, while the Wahhabi state of central Arabia pressed in from the interior. Qataris chafed under Bahraini overlordship, and a sense of distinct identity hardened through these disputes over tribute, ports and tribal allegiance.

Conflict came to a head in 1867, when Bahraini and allied forces attacked Al Wakrah and Al Bidda (in the area of modern Doha), destroying homes and deporting inhabitants. The British, who maintained a maritime peace in the Gulf, regarded this assault as a breach of earlier agreements. In its aftermath the British Political Resident, Colonel Lewis Pelly, dealt directly with the Qatari leader. On 12 September 1868 Pelly concluded a separate agreement with Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani, treating him as the responsible authority on the peninsula.

This 1868 agreement is widely taken to mark the moment Qatar emerged as a distinct political entity, separate from Bahrain, and to set the course for the rule of the Al Thani dynasty, who until then had been only one among several important families. It did not yet make Qatar a protectorate, but it recognised the Al Thani as the power Britain would deal with. Within a few years a new external force arrived: in 1871 the Ottoman Empire, expanding its presence in eastern Arabia, established a garrison on the peninsula.

events.title: Bahraini attack and the Qatari-Bahraini conflict
events.summary: Forces from Bahrain and its allies attacked Al Wakrah and Al Bidda, an assault the British viewed as a breach of Gulf agreements and that sharpened Qatar's sense of separate identity.

events.title: British agreement with Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani
events.summary: Colonel Lewis Pelly concluded a separate agreement with Mohammed bin Thani, widely taken to mark Qatar's emergence as a distinct entity and the rise of Al Thani rule.

events.title: Ottoman garrison established
events.summary: The Ottoman Empire extended its presence into eastern Arabia and placed a garrison on the Qatari peninsula, beginning several decades of nominal Ottoman authority.

figures.name: Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani
figures.role: First Al Thani ruler of the Qatari peninsula
figures.blurb: Recognised by Britain in the 1868 agreement, he is regarded as the first of the Al Thani to lead the whole peninsula, founding the dynasty that still rules Qatar.

eras.title: Between the Ottomans and Britain: The 1916 Protectorate
eras.standfirst: Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed, founder of modern Qatar, balanced Ottoman and British power and defeated an Ottoman force in 1893; in 1916 his son made Qatar a British protectorate.
body: Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani, son of Mohammed bin Thani, formally succeeded his father on 18 December 1878 - the date now commemorated as Qatar's National Day. Regarded as the founder of the modern state, Jassim ruled for decades and worked to unify the Qatari tribes under Al Thani leadership. He had admitted the Ottoman garrison in 1871, partly out of loyalty to the Islamic caliphate and partly to resist growing British influence, and for a time accepted nominal Ottoman authority over the peninsula.

Relations with the Ottomans soured as their officials sought tighter control. In March 1893, after Ottoman commanders imprisoned Qatari leaders and marched on the Al Thani stronghold, Jassim's tribal forces met and defeated them at the Battle of Al Wajba. The victory compelled the Ottomans to release their captives and is remembered in Qatar as a defining assertion of autonomy. Ottoman presence dwindled thereafter, and as the First World War broke up the empire, the garrison was withdrawn, leaving the field to Britain.

On 3 November 1916 Sheikh Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani signed a treaty with Britain, ratified in 1918, that made Qatar a British protectorate. By its terms the ruler agreed to have no relations with any other power, and to grant no pearl-fishery or other concessions, without British consent, in return for British protection - an arrangement resembling Britain's agreements with the other Gulf shaikhdoms. In 1935 Abdullah signed an oil concession, opening the peninsula to the prospecting that would soon remake it.

events.title: Sheikh Jassim succeeds as ruler
events.summary: Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani formally succeeded his father, an event commemorated as Qatar's National Day and marking the consolidation of the tribes under Al Thani leadership.

events.title: Battle of Al Wajba
events.summary: Sheikh Jassim's tribal forces defeated an Ottoman expedition at Al Wajba, forcing the release of Qatari captives in a victory remembered as a key assertion of Qatari autonomy.

events.title: Anglo-Qatari Treaty: British protectorate
events.summary: Sheikh Abdullah bin Jassim signed a treaty (ratified 1918) placing Qatar under British protection in exchange for foregoing relations and concessions with other powers without British consent.

events.title: First oil concession signed
events.summary: Sheikh Abdullah bin Jassim granted an oil concession, opening Qatar to the exploration that would lead to the discovery of oil within a few years.

figures.name: Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani
figures.blurb: The founding figure of the Qatari state, he unified the tribes, admitted then resisted the Ottomans, and won the Battle of Al Wajba in 1893; his 1878 succession is marked as National Day.

pullquote.text: He would not have relations with, nor receive the agent of, any other Power without the consent of the High British Government.

eras.title: Oil, the End of Pearls, and Independence
eras.standfirst: The discovery of oil in 1939 and exports from 1949 began to transform a pearling economy in decline, and in 1971 Qatar became an independent state.
body: Oil was discovered in Qatar in 1939, at the Dukhan field on the west of the peninsula, but the outbreak of the Second World War halted development for most of the 1940s. The timing was harsh: the natural-pearl trade, Qatar's economic mainstay, had already collapsed in the 1930s under the twin blows of the global depression and the cultured pearls produced in Japan. For a decade the peninsula endured real hardship before petroleum could take the pearl's place as the source of livelihood.

Commercial oil exports finally began in 1949, the first cargo of Dukhan crude shipped from the port that became Umm Said (Mesaieed). Revenues rose quickly and reshaped Qatari society, funding the first schools, hospitals and infrastructure, but they also stirred bitter disputes within the Al Thani family over how the new wealth should be shared. Britain, still the protecting power, intervened in the succession at mid-century, and the oil era set in motion the rivalries that would shape Qatari politics for decades.

Through the 1950s and 1960s Qatar remained a British-protected shaikhdom while Britain prepared to withdraw from east of Suez. Qatar briefly considered joining a federation of Gulf emirates that became the United Arab Emirates, but chose its own path. On 3 September 1971 Qatar declared its independence, ending the 1916 protectorate; the United States recognised the State of Qatar on 5 September and diplomatic relations followed in 1972. The young state was a monarchy under the Al Thani, its future staked on hydrocarbons.

events.title: Oil discovered at Dukhan
events.summary: Following the 1935 concession, oil was discovered at the Dukhan field, though development stalled during the Second World War.

events.title: First oil exports begin
events.summary: Commercial export of Dukhan crude began in 1949, starting the transformation of an economy battered by the collapse of the pearl trade in the 1930s.

events.title: Independence from Britain
events.summary: Qatar declared independence from Great Britain, ending the protectorate established in 1916; the United States recognised the new State of Qatar on 5 September 1971.

eras.title: The Gas Boom: Hamad, Al Jazeera and New Wealth
eras.standfirst: Under Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, who took power in 1995, Qatar developed the vast North Field gas reserves and launched Al Jazeera, becoming one of the world's wealthiest and most visible small states.
body: Independent Qatar was ruled first by Sheikh Ahmad, then from 1972 by Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, who came to power in a palace change and modernised the administration. The decisive turn came in 1995, when his son Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani - long the de facto leader and minister of defence - took power. Hamad set about an ambitious transformation, opening Qatari politics modestly with the country's first municipal elections in 1999 and largely abolishing press censorship.

Hamad's defining bet was natural gas. Qatar sits atop a share of the offshore North Field, one of the largest non-associated gas fields in the world, and from the mid-1990s the state invested heavily in liquefied natural gas. Within two decades Qatar became the world's leading exporter of LNG, and its per-capita income soared to among the highest on earth. Gas wealth funded sovereign investments abroad, the new district of Doha, world-class infrastructure and an outsized role in regional affairs far beyond the peninsula's small size.

In November 1996 Hamad founded Al Jazeera, the first independent Arabic-language satellite news channel, broadcasting from Doha. Reaching audiences across the Arab world and, after 2006, in English worldwide, Al Jazeera gave Qatar extraordinary soft power and made it both admired and resented by neighbours unused to an unfettered regional broadcaster. In June 2013, in a move unusual for the Gulf, Hamad abdicated in favour of his son Tamim, citing the need to make way for a new generation.

events.title: Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa takes power
events.summary: Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani became emir in 1995, launching reforms and the development of Qatar's natural gas wealth that would make it one of the richest states in the world.

events.title: Al Jazeera launched
events.summary: Qatar founded Al Jazeera, the first independent Arabic-language satellite news channel, broadcasting from Doha and giving the small state global reach and influence.

events.title: North Field LNG development
events.summary: Qatar invested heavily in liquefied natural gas from its offshore North Field, becoming the world's leading LNG exporter and reaching among the highest per-capita incomes globally.

events.title: Hamad abdicates in favour of Tamim
events.summary: In a transfer of power unusual for the Gulf, Sheikh Hamad abdicated in favour of his son Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, then 33, to make way for a new generation.

figures.name: Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
figures.blurb: Architect of modern Qatar's wealth and influence, he developed North Field gas, founded Al Jazeera, eased censorship and held the first municipal elections before abdicating in favour of his son in 2013.

pullquote.text: He largely abolished censorship of the press and founded Al Jazeera, a news network that attained global prominence.

eras.title: Qatar Under Tamim: Blockade and World Stage
eras.standfirst: Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani led Qatar through a four-year blockade by neighbouring states, ended in 2021, and hosted the first World Cup in the Arab world in 2022.
body: Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani became emir in June 2013 and continued the gas-funded, internationally engaged course of his father. His leadership faced its sternest test on 5 June 2017, when Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain and Egypt severed diplomatic ties and imposed a land, sea and air blockade on Qatar. The four governments accused Doha of supporting terrorism and of ties with Iran and Islamist movements, and presented a list of thirteen demands including the closure of Al Jazeera. Qatar rejected the allegations and the demands as a violation of its sovereignty and international law.

During the blockade Qatar redirected trade routes, deepened ties with Turkey and Iran, and sustained its economy on gas revenues; the dispute reshaped Gulf politics without forcing Doha to yield. The crisis was formally ended at the Gulf Cooperation Council summit held in Al Ula, Saudi Arabia, on 5 January 2021, where the parties signed a solidarity and stability declaration restoring relations and reopening air, land and sea routes. The four-year rupture, treated by all sides as a watershed, closed without Qatar conceding the core demands.

Qatar's global profile reached a peak in 2022, when it hosted the FIFA World Cup, awarded to it in December 2010 and staged from 20 November to 18 December - the first World Cup held in an Arab and Muslim country. The tournament, which drew both celebration and scrutiny over labour and social conditions, capped a decade of vast stadium, transport and urban investment. Into 2026 Qatar, under Tamim, remains a wealthy gas exporter and an active diplomatic mediator, its small peninsula carrying influence far beyond its size.

events.title: Sheikh Tamim becomes emir
events.summary: Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani succeeded his father as emir of Qatar, having earlier overseen the country's successful bid to host the 2022 World Cup.

events.title: Blockade by neighbouring states
events.summary: Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Bahrain and Egypt cut ties and imposed a land, sea and air blockade, accusing Qatar of supporting terrorism; Qatar rejected their thirteen demands as a breach of its sovereignty.

events.title: Al Ula declaration ends the blockade
events.summary: At the GCC summit in Al Ula, the parties signed a solidarity and stability agreement restoring relations and reopening routes, ending the nearly four-year dispute.

events.title: Qatar hosts the FIFA World Cup
events.summary: Qatar staged the FIFA World Cup, the first in an Arab and Muslim country, after a decade of major infrastructure investment, drawing both global celebration and scrutiny.

figures.name: Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.blurb: Emir since 2013, he steered Qatar through the 2017-2021 blockade and the 2022 World Cup, sustaining its gas wealth and prominent role as a regional diplomatic mediator.

figures.name: Sheikh Mohammed bin Thani
figures.role: First Al Thani ruler of the peninsula
figures.blurb: Recognised by Britain in the 1868 agreement that is taken to mark Qatar's emergence as a distinct entity, he founded the Al Thani line of rulers.

figures.name: Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani
figures.blurb: Unified the Qatari tribes and won the 1893 Battle of Al Wajba against the Ottomans; his 1878 succession is commemorated as National Day.

figures.name: Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
figures.blurb: Developed North Field gas, founded Al Jazeera and modernised the state, making Qatar one of the world's wealthiest countries before abdicating to his son in 2013.
